title: Lesson 371
transcript: Physical security. Physical security is absolutely integral to the security of an organization. And physical security controls start all the way from outside the perimeter of a building to all the way within the building. And physical security is, as I mentioned, of paramount importance to an organization. If an organization wants to achieve the three goals of security that we've talked about, right? Confidentiality, integrity, and availability. Physical security is... critical to achieving all these things. Availability, for instance. Without physical security providing a good, clean, consistent supply of power and network connection and, especially in a data center, air at the right temperature and the right humidity, you're not going to have systems that are running. If you don't have a good clean supply of electricity in environmental systems, you're not going to have systems that are providing the integrity that you need. And physical security is of paramount importance to confidentiality as well. There's an expression that I love in physical security, which is, if you can touch the box, you own the box. In other words, if someone can gain physical access to a system, It's usually trivially easy to gain access to that system. Remember earlier we talked about bypass controls. Bypass controls are intentionally built into a system. And a lot of bypass controls are very easy to access if you can get physical access to a device. If you can touch the box, you own the box. So physical security is of paramount importance to achieving logical security. Now, when it relates to the goals of physical security, there's one thing you should remember. There's one primary goal of physical security that trumps everything else, that is most important above anything else. What do you think that is? Safety of people. Safety of people is the most important goal in physical security. For good reason, right? I mean, ethically it's the correct thing to do. People are the most valuable asset of the organization, and you can't run an organization without people. And so, the most important thing to do in physical security is ensure the safety of people and that overrides this most important primary goal of physical security the safety of people overrides many other controls and i'll show you some examples of that later on in this domain Now, the controls that we have in physical security are named slightly different than the logical security controls. You'll recall the logical security controls were directive, deterrent, preventative, detective, corrective, and recovery, and compensating. Slightly differently named in physical security, we have deter slash prevent, delay, detect, assess, and respond. But if you recall, when we talked about the logical security controls, we talked about how what you always want to have at a minimum is a complete control. A complete control is at a minimum preventive, detective, and corrective. And it is exactly the same underlying concept that we want to have in physical security. We always want to prevent as many risks as we possibly can, but we can't prevent everything. So therefore we need good detective controls, so we can detect when a risk has occurred, and then, if we've detected a risk that occurs, we want to correct it. So at a minimum, we always want preventive, detective, and corrective. It's exactly the same concept in physical security. Deter, prevent, that's your preventive control. Detect, it's your detective control. And then assessing and responding, that's how we correct in physical security. By assessing what's happened and responding to it, that's how we correct. So it's the same underlying concept. Now you'll notice that there was one control I skipped over here. Delay controls. There's lots of different things that are considered delay controls that slow someone down in physical security. The textbook example of a delay control that you should remember are locks. We'll talk about this more when we talk about locks, but locks are considered a delay control because there has never yet been an unpickable lock created. Locks just slow someone down, so they're considered delay control. Now, the major sort of categories of threats that we have to physical security are natural environmental utility systems and human-made political events. So when we're talking about natural environmental threats, what we're talking about here are acts of nature, force majeure. So things like hurricanes, floods, tornadoes, earthquakes. These are all major environmental threats that can have a huge impact on a building and the operations of a building. So we're concerned with any environmental threats. In physical security, we're very concerned with any threats to the three core utilities, power, network, and HVAC, heating, ventilation, and air conditioning. And then kind of the final major category of threat that we're concerned with is human-made political events. I mean, when we're filming this right now in, what is it, 2022? Is it 2022? Okay. It's getting late in the day here. I'm not even sure what year it is. I mean, in 2022 right now, there's some pretty horrific stuff happening in the world. The war between Russia and Ukraine is happening. There's some pretty amazing, crazy stuff happening in Iran right now. These sort of really tragic events. I mean, obviously the biggest concern is what's happening to the people. This also has a big impact on business and their operations. So, physical security is concerned about these sorts of things. In a slightly lighter view on things, take a look at this picture here. Anyone, do you recognize what major event this is from? If you're a hockey fan, you might recognize it. This is a few years ago when the Vancouver Canucks lost the Stanley Cup to those bastards, the Boston Bruins. People in Vancouver lost their minds. And the reason I like to show this picture is I was actually living in Vancouver at this time. And I was right in the middle of this insane riot of people lighting cars on fire and crazy stuff because their team lost a hockey game. By the way, that's me that's up the telephone pole. I decided that was a good place to go after I lit the car on fire. But actually, the reason I like to show this photo is that at the time, one of my clients was a major telecom provider. And they had a really high-end data center just a few blocks from where this picture was taken. And that data center was actually knocked offline as a direct result of a hockey team losing a hockey game. What happened was in the alley beside the data center, another car had been lit on fire. And up above the car in the alley were some transformers. And if you're familiar with transformers, they're typically cooled with oil. If you get a transformer hot enough, they literally explode. And that's exactly what happened. Three of these transformers popped. blew up and that's caused such a massive shock of electricity that it just fried the equipment in this data center. And, you know, that was despite the fact that this was a really high-end data center. They had all sorts of proper electrical equipment to try and make sure that, you know, lightning strikes things like that wouldn't take out their equipment but this was just such a massive surge in power that it ended up knocking out a ton of their equipment i was actually in the data center a few days after this happened the whole whole place like reeked of ozone there was just so much fried electrical equipment so these are the sorts of things physical security is concerned with Some additional threats to physical security, a couple that we have to highlight here, dumpster diving. This is where you're going through someone's trash. A more modern version of this, go on eBay, look for equipment that companies are disposing of, retiring. You can find all sorts of highly sensitive data. There was a police department in the US that sold off some big Xerox printers and they forgot to wipe or remove the hard drives from these printers. And so when you go to make a copy of a bunch of pages on a printer, it will store those pages so it can make multiple copies. Police station didn't think about this, sold their printers on eBay, and there was hundreds of thousands of highly sensitive police records that was on these printers. They had to declare a privacy breach. The other one I'll hit on here is shoulder surfing. Shoulder surfing is where you look over someone's shoulder to see what's on their screen. The modern day example of this one is a lot of companies nowadays are putting these really high resolution 4K cameras in their buildings and some of them are like pzt cameras pan zoom and tilt i have another fun story on this one there was actually a pen test so i was managing a team that was conducting a pen test And one of the guys on my team sent me a link and he's like, dude, you gotta check this out. And to be very clear, I had a signed NDA, et cetera. It was appropriate for me to be looking at this. But when I checked the link, it was a link to a really high resolution PZT security camera in the back of the security operations center of the company that we were performing this pen test on. So we were actually able to like zoom into the analyst screens and watch what alerts they were getting on their screens and checking to see if the, you know, the actions that we were performing the pen test were generating alerts on the analyst screen. So that was obviously not great. So it's kind of a modern day example of shoulder surfing. One more little piece that I'll cover before we wrap up this video. And that's just a concept that we've covered before, but I'll reiterate here. It's this concept of defense in depth or layered security. And this is that basic idea that we never just want to have a single layer of defense. We want to have multiple layers of defense. And at each layer, we want to have a complete control, preventive, detective, and corrective. So in physical security, often the first layer of defense will be the perimeter, the fence around the building. The fence will be your preventive control. Then maybe things like electromagnet or sorry, optical tripwires or microwave tripwires or there's sensors that you can install in fences to detect if they're being cut. That could be your detective control. And then your corrective control might be releasing the hungry dogs or getting a security guard to go out and look at what is happening. So the first layer of defense around the building's perimeter would be the fencing. And then your next layer of defense are probably the walls of the building. the walls will be the preventative control things like camera systems will be your detective control and then guards or something like that will again be your corrective control and that's you know it's your layers of defense aren't just going to end at the perimeter like the the walls of the building In a secure facility, you'll have often multiple additional layers of defense, additional doors and sort of zones that someone has to go through to get to, say, the data center floor. And at each layer, you want to have preventive, detective, and corrective controls. Now, there's one more fun little thing I want to ask you here. I'll back up a second here. It's a fun question, Mil, I want you to think about for a second. What do you think would be the most secure number of entrances and exits to have in and out of the building? John, what do you think? What's the most secure number? Uh, zero. Zero? Yeah, you're right. It's like you've done this before, John. Yeah, zero would be the most secure number. Not very functional though, right? Only having no, if you had no entrances and exits. So you're going to want to have at least one entrance and exit. But here's kind of interesting a point to make. Almost no buildings are only going to have a single entrance and exit. Why? Well, what do we prioritize above all else in physical security? Safety of people. And that's why even in really secure facilities, you'll have all sorts of fire doors around the perimeter of the building. Does that make the building less secure? Yes, but it's all about prioritizing the safety of people. If there's a fire, you want to make sure that people can get out as easily as possible. And so you'll have fire doors. It's required by fire laws usually, right? So this is a great example of how we prioritize the safety of people above all else.
